items.question: What is supervised learning in one sentence?
items.answer: Supervised learning trains a model on labeled (x, y) pairs to minimize prediction error against known targets y, then predicts ŷ for new inputs x without labels.

items.question: What is the difference between supervised and unsupervised learning?
items.answer: Supervised learning uses labeled training data (each x has a known y); unsupervised learning uses unlabeled x only and finds structure without per-example targets.

items.question: Give two examples of supervised learning tasks — one regression, one classification.
items.answer: Regression: predict house price from features with past sale prices as labels. Classification: spam detection with emails labeled spam or ham.

items.question: Why do you need labels on a validation or test set in supervised learning?
items.answer: Metrics like accuracy, F1, or MSE compare predictions ŷ to true y. Without held-out labels you cannot measure whether the model generalizes beyond memorizing training data.

items.question: What happens at inference time in a supervised model — are labels required?
items.answer: No. Inference takes only feature vector x, runs ŷ = f_θ(x), and returns the prediction. Labels are used during training and evaluation, not at serving time.

items.question: How does label leakage undermine supervised learning?
items.answer: If features contain information only available after y is known, the model learns shortcuts that work on training/validation splits but fail in production when those features are missing or delayed.
